---
title: "Lab 8"
subtitle: "Nonparametric A/B Forensics: When the Mean Is the Suspect"
author: "Ihor Miroshnychenko"
institute: Kyiv School of Economics
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# Objective

In **Lab 7** you learned that a tidy "chi-square is significant" line can hide
six different realities. **Lab 8 returns to a numeric metric — delivery time —
and takes away the crutch you have leaned on since Lab 4: the mean.** Product
metrics like delivery minutes are heavily right-skewed with the odd
stuck-in-traffic outlier, and the platform's headline verdict — a **Welch
$t$-test on the means** — is exactly the test that skew and outliers break.

The GreenBowl experimentation platform A/B-tested a new **courier dispatcher**.
For every order it logged the **delivery time in minutes** (lower = better) and
the **courier** who carried it. The automated report prints a Welch-$t$
"ship / don't ship" line. Your job is to decide whether you can **believe** it.

The lecture gave you a family of tests that read only the **direction** or the
**order** of the data, plus the guardrails around them:

- **Sign test** — keep only the direction of each change (binomial); also the
  one-sample test of a median against a target KPI.
- **Wilcoxon signed-rank** — rank the sizes of *paired* changes; needs
  **symmetric** differences.
- **Mann–Whitney U** — two independent groups compared by ranks; its $H_0$ is
  $F = G$, **not** "the medians are equal".
- **Effect sizes** — the probability of superiority $\hat P(A<B) = U/NM$ and
  the **Hodges–Lehmann** shift *in minutes*, with a distribution-free CI.
- **Brunner–Munzel** — the honest rank test when the two groups have
  **different shapes** (Practice 8's "shape trap").
- **Monte Carlo** — the course-long habit: validate whatever criterion your
  verdict rests on, at *your* $n$ and *your* kind of data.

The twist, as in Labs 4–7: **you do not get to see what is wrong.** Your
experiment is pulled live from `greenbowl_mu.py`, keyed to your student id.
Some students received a genuine, business-meaningful speed-up; some an honest
null; some a mean "win" manufactured by a handful of **outliers**; some a
microscopic shift made "significant" by an enormous sample; some a dispatcher
that keeps the **median** but wrecks the **tail**; and some a **crossover**
design where the same couriers appear in both arms — so every
independent-samples test is invalid before it starts. **The only honest way to
find out which one you hold is to run the diagnostics.**

You will:

1. Pull your experiment and read the platform's **headline Welch-$t$ verdict**
   — the one you are about to put on trial.
2. Run **design & shape forensics**: is the design *really* two independent
   groups? What does the metric's distribution look like, and can the mean be
   trusted with it?
3. Warm up on the **SLA sign test** — one sample against the contractual
   45-minute median.
4. Build the **rank toolkit** step by step — `mw_report` → `ab_report` →
   `robust_ab` — or, if your design turns out to be paired, the
   `sign_test` / `signed_rank` pair on within-courier differences.
5. Check the **shape trap**: what Mann–Whitney's $H_0$ really claims, and when
   Brunner–Münzel (or plain quantiles) is the honest answer.
6. **Validate with Monte Carlo** — the FPR and power of the exact criterion
   your verdict rests on, at your $n$.
7. Deliver a one-page **forensic verdict** a non-statistician PM can act on.

# General Requirements

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## The fixed business context (same for everyone)

- **Unit:** one order.
- **Metric:** **delivery time, minutes** — *lower is better*.
- **Arms:** `control` = old dispatcher, `test` = new dispatcher.
- **SLA:** the courier contract promises a **median ≤ 45 min** (`SLA_MIN`).
- **Business threshold:** a median speed-up smaller than **3 minutes**
  (`BUSINESS_MDE`) is not worth the rollout risk.
- **Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Everyone's experiment is different — on purpose

Your experiment is unique to your id. It might be a clean win, an honest null,
an outlier artifact dressed up as a win, a significant-but-trivial shift on a
huge sample, a same-median-fatter-tail reliability regression, or a paired
crossover that independent tests cannot even legally touch. **There is no
single "right answer" shared across the class** — there is only the diagnosis
your diagnostics support. Copying another student's conclusion will be
obviously wrong for your experiment.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Report Format

Submit the report in **one** of the following formats:

- **Quarto → PDF** (`.qmd` → `.pdf`)
- **Quarto → HTML** (`.qmd` → `.html`, hosted, e.g. on
  [Quarto Pub](https://quartopub.com/),
  [Posit Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/))
- **Jupyter Notebook** (`.ipynb`)
- **Google Colab** (share the link with viewing permissions)

The report must contain both code **and** narrative. Every section begins with
a written explanation, not code.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## What you build vs. what you use ready-made

The thing you **build** today is the **rank-based guardrail toolkit** —
`design_check`, `sign_test`, `mw_report` → `ab_report` → `robust_ab`, and the
Monte-Carlo validators — grown step by step from Practice 8, each version
**reusing** the previous one. The **criteria** themselves you take ready-made:
`scipy.stats.mannwhitneyu`, `scipy.stats.wilcoxon`, `scipy.stats.binomtest`,
`scipy.stats.brunnermunzel`, `scipy.stats.ttest_ind` / `ttest_rel`, and
`statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint` for the Wilson interval. You
may **not** replace your validators with a library power/AA function — the
simulation loop and the confidence-interval verdict must be **your own code**.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Honour rule on the data platform

`greenbowl_mu.py` contains the hidden truth in its source. **Do not read past
the `# === instructor section ===` line, and do not call `_reveal`.** The point
of the lab is to reach the diagnosis *by running the diagnostics*, not by
reading the answer. Any report that names the hidden pathology from the source
instead of demonstrating it with the toolkit scores **zero** for
interpretation.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Deadline

📅 **July 8, 2026, 23:59**
:::

# Tasks

## Get Your Experiment {#sec-data}

Download `greenbowl_mu.py` (provided with the lab) into your working folder
and load **your** experiment. Use **one** id consistently — your KSE email is
recommended.

```{python}
#| label: load
#| eval: false

from greenbowl_mu import load_my_experiment, ALPHA, SLA_MIN, BUSINESS_MDE
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import (mannwhitneyu, wilcoxon, binomtest, brunnermunzel,
                         ttest_ind, ttest_rel, ttest_1samp, rankdata)
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint

STUDENT_ID = "your.email@kse.org.ua"   # <-- put YOUR id here
exp = load_my_experiment(STUDENT_ID)

print(exp)                     # arm sizes
print(exp.data.head())         # one row per order: courier_id, arm, minutes
control, test = exp.control, exp.test
```

::: {.border}
1. State your `STUDENT_ID`. Report $n_{\text{control}}$, $n_{\text{test}}$, and
   a small numeric summary of each arm: mean, median, min, max, P90.
2. Run the platform's **headline test** — `ttest_ind(control, test,
   equal_var=False)` — and write down the "ship / don't ship / roll back" line
   it would print (remember: *lower minutes = better*, so a significantly
   **smaller test mean** reads as "ship"). **This is the verdict you are about
   to put on trial** — do not believe it yet.
3. Confirm in one sentence that you understand the experiment's true nature is
   **hidden**, and your task is to *diagnose* it with the rank toolkit.
:::

## Design & Shape Forensics (`design_check`) {#sec-design}

Before choosing *any* test, answer two questions the headline never asks.
**First: what is the design?** Independent-samples tests (Welch $t$,
Mann–Whitney) require the two arms to be **independent** — no shared users, no
repeated measures. If the *same courier* appears in both arms, you have a
**crossover** and must switch to the paired family. **Second: what is the
shape?** If the metric is heavily skewed, the mean answers a question nobody
asked.

::: {.border}
1. Implement **Version 1** of your toolkit, `design_check(exp)`, that returns:
   whether any `courier_id` appears in **both arms** (→ paired!), the arm
   sizes, and per-arm skew diagnostics (mean vs median gap, P90/P50 ratio).
   *Hint: `exp.data["courier_id"].duplicated().any()`, or compare the sets of
   ids per arm.*
2. Run it. **State your design**: independent A/B or paired crossover. This
   single boolean decides which half of the lecture applies to you — and
   whether the platform's headline test was even *legal*.
3. Plot the two arms' distributions (overlaid histograms **and** an ECDF
   plot). Describe the shape in one sentence: skew, outliers, tails. Mark the
   means and medians on the histogram — how far apart are they, and why?
4. In one sentence: given this shape, what could make a **mean-based** verdict
   lie, and which summaries would you trust instead?
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Research hook
Most students will find an honest independent design here — and that finding
is worth one sentence, not zero: "I checked, no courier appears twice, the
independent-samples family applies." For a few of you this check will
**overturn the entire analysis**. Skipping it and being wrong is the single
most expensive mistake in this lab.
:::

## Warm-up: the SLA Sign Test (`sign_test`) {#sec-sla}

The courier contract promises a **median delivery time ≤ 45 min** in the
control (current) experience. Before any A/B question, the platform must
verify the SLA — and the right tool for "is the median above the target?" on
a skewed metric with zero distributional assumptions is the **one-sample sign
test**: count how many orders exceed the target and compare with a fair coin,
$T = \#\{x_i > m_0\} \sim \text{Binom}(n, \tfrac12)$ under $H_0$.

::: {.border}
1. Implement `sign_test_median(x, m0, alternative="greater", alpha=0.05)` using
   `scipy.stats.binomtest` (drop exact ties with `m0` first). Return the count
   above target, $n$, the $p$-value, and a verdict.
2. Run it on your **control** arm against `SLA_MIN`: test $H_1$: "median > 45
   min". Report the sample median next to the verdict.
3. Interpret for the contract owner in one sentence — and be careful about the
   direction of the logic: "not rejected" means *consistent with the SLA*, not
   *proven to meet it* (recall the lecture's KPI example).
:::

## The Right Test for Your Design (`mw_report` → `ab_report`) {#sec-test}

Now the headline question, answered with the machinery your design permits.
**Follow the branch your @sec-design verdict dictates** — and in both branches,
report an **effect size in minutes**, not just a $p$-value.

### Branch A — independent A/B (most students)

The rank answer to "did the new dispatcher speed deliveries up?" is
**Mann–Whitney** plus two effect sizes: the **probability of superiority**
$\hat P(\text{test} < \text{control})$ — the share of random order pairs where
the test order arrives sooner — and the **Hodges–Lehmann shift** — the median
of all pairwise differences, in minutes, with its distribution-free CI (the
inversion of the U-test from the lecture).

::: {.border}
1. Implement **Version 2**, `mw_report(control, test, alpha=0.05)`: run
   `mannwhitneyu`, return $U$, the $p$-value,
   $\hat P(\text{test} < \text{control})$, and a verdict. (Mind the direction:
   `scipy` reports $U$ for its **first** argument; check which orientation of
   $U/NM$ gives "test is faster" and say so in a comment.)
2. Implement **Version 3**, `ab_report(control, test, alpha=0.05,
   mde=BUSINESS_MDE)`, that **reuses** `mw_report` and adds the
   **Hodges–Lehmann** shift with its 95% CI (lecture code:
   `np.subtract.outer` → sorted pairwise differences → the rank-quantile
   indices). If an arm is longer than ~2 000 observations, compute HL on a
   **random subsample** of 2 000 per arm (the $NM$ pairwise matrix does not
   fit in memory at 150k×150k) — and say you did.
3. Run `ab_report`. Report $p$, $\hat P(\text{test}<\text{control})$, the HL
   shift in minutes with its CI, and — separately — the **decision layer**: is
   the shift, *at its CI's optimistic and pessimistic ends*, past
   `BUSINESS_MDE`? "Statistically real" and "worth shipping" are two different
   sentences; write both.
4. Put your rank verdict **next to the headline Welch $t$** from @sec-data. If
   they agree, say what that adds. If they disagree, this is the heart of your
   lab: find *why* (re-plot; try the comparison with the most extreme 1–2% of
   values winsorised; look at where mean and median moved). Which verdict do
   you trust, and what exactly is the other one reacting to?
:::

### Branch B — paired crossover (if `design_check` found shared couriers)

The arms share couriers, so **independent tests are void** — including the
platform's headline. Collapse each courier to their difference
$d_i = \text{control}_i - \text{test}_i$ (positive = the new dispatcher made
courier $i$ faster) and test *that*, exactly as in Practice 8 Part I–II.

::: {.border}
1. Build the per-courier paired frame (`exp.data.pivot(index="courier_id",
   columns="arm", values="minutes")`) and compute the differences $d_i$.
2. Implement `sign_test(before, after)` and `signed_rank(before, after)` (reuse
   the Practice 8 versions), plus the paired $t$ (`ttest_rel`) for contrast.
   Run all three, one-sided ("the new dispatcher is faster").
3. **Check the symmetry** of the $d_i$ histogram before trusting signed-rank —
   if it is visibly asymmetric, the sign test is your primary verdict, and say
   so.
4. Report the **median per-courier saving in minutes** and the share of
   couriers who improved. Decision layer: is the saving past `BUSINESS_MDE`?
   Be honest if it is *statistically clear but sitting on the business
   threshold* — that is a "ship, but re-measure at scale" recommendation, not
   a triumphant one.
5. Explain in two sentences why the pooled Welch $t$ (and a pooled
   Mann–Whitney) **missed** what the paired analysis sees — where did the
   between-courier variance go?
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Stop-the-line rule
If your design is paired and you report a pooled independent-samples $p$-value
anyway, everything downstream is void — the observations are not independent,
so the test's null distribution is simply not the one it was calibrated on.
The design check is not bureaucracy; it decides which mathematics exists for
you.
:::

## The Shape Trap (`robust_ab`) {#sec-shape}

Mann–Whitney's strict null is $F = G$ — the **whole distributions** match. It
reads cleanly as a **median/shift** test only when the two shapes are the
same. If the new dispatcher changes the *spread* (some orders much earlier,
some much later), then: (a) the **means** move even when medians do not (a
lognormal's mean grows with its variance) — fooling the headline $t$; (b)
$P(A<B)$ can sit at exactly $0.5$ — so **Mann–Whitney sees nothing**; and (c)
with **unequal group sizes**, MW's calibration itself breaks (Practice 8, Part
IV). A "no change" from MW plus a "slower!" from Welch $t$ can both be wrong
*about the thing the business cares about*.

::: {.border}
1. Compare the two arms' **shape**, not just location: the variance (or IQR)
   ratio, and a quantile table — P10 / P50 / P90 per arm. Overlay the two
   ECDFs. Do the curves *shift* or *rotate* (cross)?
2. Run `brunnermunzel(control, test)` next to your `mw_report`. Recall from
   Practice 8: BM tests stochastic superiority while staying calibrated when
   shapes and variances differ (and MW's own FPR can drift far from 5% with
   unequal $n$ and unequal spreads — your Monte-Carlo section can prove this
   for *your* sizes).
3. Implement **Version 4**, `robust_ab(exp, alpha=0.05, mde=BUSINESS_MDE)` —
   your all-in-one entry point, which **reuses** everything so far:
   `design_check` first (paired → route to the sign/signed-rank branch);
   otherwise compare shapes (variance/IQR ratio and the quantile table); if
   shapes clearly differ, report **Brunner–Münzel + quantiles** as the honest
   reading; else `ab_report`. Use `robust_ab` as your final analytical word.
4. **If your quantile table shows the tail moving while the median stands
   still** — say it in business language: the median customer is unaffected,
   but the P90 customer waits X minutes instead of Y, and SLA breaches went
   from a% to b% (compute the share of orders above `SLA_MIN` per arm). Which
   headline — "ship", "no change", "roll back" — does *that* support, and is
   it any of the ones the tests printed?
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Research hook
One of the hidden worlds has **equal medians and $P(A<B) = 0.5$ exactly** — a
rank test *cannot* see it, by construction, and the mean test "sees" it for
the wrong reason. If every test you ran feels weirdly contradictory, stop
testing and look at the two ECDFs. The answer is a picture, not a $p$-value.
:::

## Validate Your Criterion with Monte Carlo {#sec-mc}

Whatever your verdict rests on — MW, BM, the sign test on differences — it is
itself a **criterion**, and the course-long habit applies: check that it keeps
its promised 5% false positives on data *shaped like yours*, at *your* sample
sizes, and measure what it *could* have detected. Reuse the `rate` / `prate`
engines from Practice 8 (rebuild them briefly in your report; they are your
own code now).

::: {.border}
1. **FPR (A/A).** Simulate the null with a right-skewed law that mimics your
   control arm (`rng.lognormal(np.log(median), sigma, n)` with your arm's
   median and a plausible sigma), at **your** $(n_{\text{control}},
   n_{\text{test}})$, 2 000+ experiments, and report the FPR of your primary
   criterion **with a Wilson CI**. Is $0.05$ inside?
2. **Stress the calibration the way your data stresses it.** Pick the stress
   relevant to *your* experiment: unequal spreads at **unequal $n$** for MW
   vs. Brunner–Münzel (the Practice 8 Part-IV table, at your sizes); or an
   A/A with a few injected extreme outliers for Welch $t$ vs. MW; or — if you
   are in the paired branch — asymmetric null differences for signed-rank vs.
   the sign test. Show the FPR table and say which criterion stays honest.
3. **Power / MDE.** Inject a *real* effect of the kind your world suggests (a
   multiplicative median shift for location; your observed per-courier lift if
   paired) and estimate the power at your $n$; then find (roughly) the
   **smallest shift in minutes** your setup detects with power ≥ 80%. If your
   verdict was "no effect", this is the line that makes it *informative*:
   "we would have seen anything ≥ X min".
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Match the validation to *your* diagnosis
A student in the outlier world should be validating *robustness to outliers*;
a student with unequal arm sizes and different spreads should be validating
*MW's FPR at those sizes*; a student in the paired world should be validating
the *paired* criteria on asymmetric differences; a student with a null should
be reporting *power*, not just a passing FPR. A generic copy-pasted A/A that
ignores what your data looks like earns the methodology point only halfway.
:::

## Final Forensic Verdict {#sec-final}

::: {.border}
Write a **single-page memo** (≤ 400 words) to a **non-technical** GreenBowl PM
who has only seen the platform's headline Welch-$t$ line. It must contain:

1. The **headline verdict** (what the automated report claimed) and your
   **one-line forensic verdict** — one of: a real, business-meaningful
   speed-up (**ship**); an honest null with a stated detectable-effect floor
   (**don't ship, and stop the experiment**); a mean artifact from stuck-order
   outliers (**don't ship; fix the headline's metric**); a
   **significant-but-trivial** shift (**don't ship**); a same-median,
   fatter-tail **reliability regression** (**don't ship; the tail, not the
   average, is the story**); or a paired crossover the platform analysed with
   the wrong test (**re-analyse paired; then decide — state your paired
   verdict**).
2. The **evidence** that forces it, quoted as numbers, not adjectives: the
   design check, the shape summary, the $p$-values you trust *and* the ones
   you overrule, $\hat P(\text{test}<\text{control})$, the Hodges–Lehmann
   shift (or median per-courier saving) in **minutes** with its CI against the
   **3-minute** business threshold, the quantile/SLA table where relevant, and
   the Monte-Carlo calibration/power at your $n$.
3. A concrete **recommendation**: ship / don't ship / fix the metric in the
   headline report / re-run the analysis paired / collect more — whichever
   your diagnosis demands.
4. One honest **caveat** of your own audit (e.g. MW answers "stochastically
   faster", not "the medians differ", unless shapes match; HL on a subsample
   is an approximation; a sign test throws away magnitude; simulation noise at
   your `n_exps`).

It must be readable by someone who has never heard the words "Mann–Whitney"
or "$p$-value". You are graded on whether the PM could act on it without
follow-up questions.
:::

# Grading {.unnumbered}

| Criterion | Points |
|---|:---:|
| Correctness of methodology | 3 |
| Interpretation of results | 3 |
| Code quality and organization | 2 |
| **Total** | **8** |

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Grading Details

**Correctness of methodology (3 pts):**

- `design_check` and the SLA `sign_test` are correct, and the analysis follows
  the branch the design dictates (paired data never fed to independent tests)
  (1 pt).
- The rank toolkit is built step by step (`mw_report` → `ab_report` →
  `robust_ab`, each version **reusing** the previous), with correct directions
  ($U$ orientation, one- vs two-sided) and a correct Hodges–Lehmann shift + CI
  (subsampled where necessary) (1 pt).
- The Monte-Carlo validation is your own loop, returns FPR/power **with** a
  Wilson CI, and stresses the criterion the way *your* data does (1 pt).

**Interpretation of results (3 pts):**

- @sec-design and @sec-shape actually gate the analysis (a paired design or a
  shape difference changes which test you trust, not just adds a sentence).
- @sec-test separates **statistically real** from **worth shipping**
  ($\hat P$, HL in minutes vs. the 3-minute threshold), and disagreements
  between the mean and rank verdicts are explained mechanically, not waved at.
- The memo's forensic verdict **matches the evidence** and overturns or
  confirms the headline for the right reason.

**Code quality and organization (2 pts):**

- The toolkit functions are single reusable pieces used across all sections
  (built step by step, not re-implemented inline).
- Plots (histogram + ECDF, symmetry histogram if paired, FPR/power curves)
  have axis labels, titles, legends, and a dashed line at $\alpha$ or at the
  business threshold where relevant.
- Narrative precedes every code block; each section reads as an argument, not
  a dump of outputs.
:::

# Defense {.unnumbered}

During the defense (5–7 minutes) you will be asked *"What if?"* questions that
probe whether you understand **why** each piece exists, for example:

- *"Your Welch $t$ says the new dispatcher is faster and Mann–Whitney says
  nothing. Show me — live — the two or three orders that explain the
  disagreement, and tell me which verdict I should believe."*
- *"Your MW $p$ is $10^{-8}$ on 300 000 orders and the Hodges–Lehmann shift is
  $-0.3$ minutes. Ship or not? What single number settles it?"*
- *"The same courier id appears in both arms. What exactly breaks in
  `mannwhitneyu` — which assumption, and what happens to its null
  distribution?"*
- *"Medians are equal, $P(A<B) = 0.5$, yet you told the PM 'don't ship'. What
  did you see that the rank test is blind to, by construction?"*
- *"Why did you trust the sign test over the signed-rank here? What did the
  histogram of differences show, and which extra assumption does signed-rank
  quietly add?"*
- *"Your A/A says MW's FPR is 11% at your group sizes. Which of your reported
  $p$-values does that invalidate, and what do you switch to?"*

You may also be asked to **change one thing live** (winsorise the outliers,
swap MW for Brunner–Münzel, pool the paired data and watch the effect vanish,
shrink $n$ in the Monte-Carlo) and explain the resulting shift.
